---
title: "Macroevolution"
---

*Macroevolutionary patterns across space and time*

![](images/proyectos/macroevolucion.jpg){fig-alt="Macroevolution" style="width:100%;max-height:320px;object-fit:cover;border-radius:6px;margin-bottom:1.5rem;"}

---

Along with the description and evaluation of geographical biodiversity patterns, our research focuses on investigating the actual processes that change species numbers within and across the planet: evolutionary processes related to speciation, extinction and dispersal (Alves et al. 2017; Pinto-Ledezma et al. 2017, 2019). We are also interested in the (macro)evolution of species traits as related to macroecological patterns such as body size gradients (Velasco et al. 2020; Rubalcaba et al. 2022) or habitat preferences (Rodrigues et al. 2019; Esparza-Estrada et al. 2022, 2023).

Evaluating macroevolutionary processes at broad spatial scales has become commonplace in macroecology and several techniques are now applied to the study of evolutionary diversification under a geographic perspective (see a review in Villalobos et al. 2020, in Rull V., Carnaval A. (eds) *Neotropical Diversification: Patterns and Processes*. Springer).

Given the nature of both macroecological and macroevolutionary patterns driven by species traits and their evolutionary history, we rely heavily on Phylogenetic Comparative Methods (PCMs). For this same reason, we are also interested in applying, testing, and sometimes developing PCMs to understand the tempo and mode of macroevolutionary and macroecological patterns (Alves et al. 2017; Bini et al. 2014; Diniz-Filho et al. 2015; Jardim et al. 2021).

## Team

Fabricio Villalobos, José Alexandre Diniz-Filho, Jesús N. Pinto-Ledezma, Julián A. Velasco, and collaborators

## Funding

- CNPq-Brazil (INCT-EECBio, UFG)
- Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología (CONACYT)

---

[← Back to Projects](proyectos.qmd)
